name: Edinburgh Castle
blurb: Gun at 1pm, Stone of Destiny, Crown Jewels, Scottish National War Memorial.

name: Mary King's Close
blurb: Warren of buried streets beneath the Royal Mile — guided tours only.

name: Royal Mile / Cockburn St
blurb: The spine of the Old Town; Cockburn St curls off it in a photogenic arc.

name: Princes St & Gardens
blurb: Valley garden below the castle rock; floral clock, the Ross Fountain.

name: Camera Obscura
blurb: Victorian rooftop camera + five floors of optical illusions.

name: Scott Monument
blurb: Gothic rocket to Walter Scott — 287 steps to the top.

name: Royal Botanic Garden
blurb: 70 acres of glasshouses and rock garden north of the New Town.

name: Heart of Midlothian
blurb: Heart-shaped causey mosaic on the High St — locals spit on it for luck.

name: Edinburgh Zoo
blurb: Penguin parade and the UK’s only koalas.

name: Canongate Kirkyard
blurb: Grave of Ebenezer Scroggie — Dickens’ ‘mean man’ → Scrooge. Also Adam Smith, Fergusson.

name: National Museum of Scotland — Lewis Chess Pieces
blurb: Some of the 12th-c. walrus-ivory Lewis chessmen live here.

name: St Giles' Cathedral
blurb: Crown spire over the Royal Mile; Thistle Chapel inside.

name: Holyrood Palace & Abbey
blurb: The King’s Scottish residence; ruined abbey alongside.

name: Scottish National Gallery
blurb: Titian, Vermeer, the Scottish collection — on the Mound.

name: Whisky cluster
blurb: Scotch Whisky Experience + Royal Mile tasting rooms.

name: Literary Walking Tour
blurb: Edinburgh Literary Pub Tour — Burns, Stevenson, Rankin. Starts Grassmarket.

name: Arthur's Seat
blurb: Extinct volcano in the middle of the city; best view in Edinburgh.

name: Calton Hill / National Monument
blurb: Edinburgh's 'unfinished Parthenon' and the postcard skyline.

name: Craigmillar Castle
blurb: Best-preserved medieval castle near the city; Mary, Queen of Scots' bolt-hole.

name: Royal Yacht Britannia
blurb: The decommissioned royal yacht, moored at Ocean Terminal.

name: Scottish Parliament
blurb: Enric Miralles’ controversial, much-awarded 2004 building.

name: Dean Village
blurb: Former milling village on the Water of Leith — a sudden pocket of quiet.

name: Isle of Skye (Fairy Glen, Sligachan)
blurb: Fairy Glen’s miniature hills + the Sligachan bridge and Cuillin views.

name: St Conan's Kirk
blurb: Eccentric lochside church stitched from every era of Scottish architecture.

name: Dunure Castle
blurb: Clifftop ruin over a tiny harbour; an Outlander filming spot.

name: Caerlaverock Castle
blurb: The only triangular castle in Britain, ringed by a moat.

name: Machir Bay (Islay)
blurb: Wide Atlantic-facing beach on whisky island Islay.

name: Cove Harbour
blurb: Hidden smugglers’ harbour reached through a cliff tunnel. (Split from Limekilns — verify.)

name: Limekilns
blurb: Forth-side village; the Bruce Inn featured in Outlander. (Split from Cove Harbour — verify.)

name: National Wallace Monument
blurb: Victorian tower over Stirling; holds Wallace’s great two-handed sword.

name: The Kelpies
blurb: Two 30m steel horse-heads by Andy Scott.

name: Falkirk Wheel
blurb: The world’s only rotating boat lift, joining two canals.

name: Black Watch Castle & Museum
blurb: Balhousie Castle, regimental home of the Black Watch.

name: Glasgow
blurb: Kelvingrove, the Mackintosh trail, the Necropolis — a city pin to break out later.

name: Carlowrie Castle
blurb: Victorian baronial house and estate near the airport.

name: Loch Ness
blurb: Deep dark loch; Urquhart Castle on its shore.

name: Balintore Castle
blurb: Victorian Gothic ruin one man is restoring single-handed.

name: Highland coos
blurb: The shaggy ginger cattle — pin a reliable roadside spot once you find one.

name: Aberdeen
blurb: The Granite City — Old Aberdeen, the harbour, gateway to Royal Deeside.

name: Hadrian's Wall
blurb: Roman frontier across England. Wall is open; forts (Housesteads) are ticketed.
hours: Forts daily (EH — seasonal); wall always open

name: Bamburgh Castle
blurb: Vast castle on a dune crag above the Northumberland coast.

name: The Shambles, York
blurb: Lurching medieval butchers’ street; the ‘Diagon Alley’ of York.

name: Newcastle (Black Gate, Dog Leap Stairs)
blurb: The castle Black Gate and the steep Dog Leap Stairs down to the Quayside.

name: Lake District (Keswick, Scafell Pike)
blurb: Keswick + Derwentwater as a base; Scafell Pike, England’s highest, to the south.

name: Manchester Bridge (Joy Division footbridge)
blurb: Epping Walk Bridge — site of Kevin Cummins’ snow-bound Joy Division shoot.

name: Haçienda site
blurb: FAC 51 — the Factory club, demolished 2002, now flats. A pilgrimage.

name: Salford Lads Club
blurb: The Smiths’ Queen Is Dead inner-sleeve doorway.

name: Macclesfield — Ian Curtis
blurb: House on Barton St, the mural, and the memorial stone in Macclesfield Cemetery.

name: Liverpool
blurb: Cavern, the Albert Dock, the whole Merseybeat map — a city pin to break out.

name: Warwick Castle
blurb: Theme-park-ish but the oubliette and the ramparts are the real medieval thing.

name: Worcestershire churches (Cradley, Colwall, Kempsey)
blurb: Three village churches — Cradley, Colwall, Kempsey. (Pinned at Cradley; split into three later.)

name: Stonehenge
blurb: The stones. Ticketed and timed — book ahead, even for the outer path.

name: Castle Combe
blurb: “The prettiest village in England” — honey-stone Cotswold cliché, earned.

name: King Alfred's Tower, Stourhead
blurb: 160ft triangular folly on the Stourhead estate edge.

name: Little Solsbury Hill, Bath
blurb: Iron-age hillfort that gave Peter Gabriel his ‘Solsbury Hill’.

name: Crawley (Robert Smith / The Cure)
blurb: Where The Cure formed. No single landmark — NME’s town guide is the reference.

name: Arundel Castle
blurb: Seat of the Dukes of Norfolk above a storybook town.

name: Merstham phone-box graveyard
blurb: A field of retired red K6 phone boxes awaiting restoration.

name: Crooked House of Windsor
blurb: Lopsided 1592 teahouse beside the castle.

name: Oxfordshire
blurb: Oxford colleges + the surrounding countryside — a county pin to break out.

name: British Museum / Rosetta Stone
blurb: Rosetta Stone, the Parthenon marbles, the Great Court roof.

name: Tate Britain / Turner
blurb: The Clore’s Turner bequest — the reason to come.

name: The Black Friar
blurb: Arts-and-Crafts pub all in marble and copper, shaped like a wedge.

name: Camden
blurb: The markets, the Roundhouse, the Amy Winehouse statue.

name: Greenwich / GMT
blurb: Stand on the Prime Meridian at the Royal Observatory.

name: Mach Loop, Machynlleth
blurb: Valley where RAF jets train low — watch fast jets below you from the ridges.

name: MOMA Machynlleth
blurb: Modern art in mid-Wales, in and around a chapel.

name: St David's Head ponies, Pembrokeshire
blurb: Wild ponies on the headland at Britain’s smallest city.

name: Skomer (puffins)
blurb: Island seabird colony — puffins up close in early summer. Boat from Martin’s Haven.

name: Denbigh Asylum
blurb: The decaying North Wales Hospital — an urbex landmark (now part-redeveloped; access restricted).

name: Isle of Man
blurb: Castle Rushen, Snaefell, the steam railways, Manx wallabies. (Pinned at Castle Rushen.)

name: Michael Kenna power stations
blurb: Cooling towers Kenna photographed: Chapelcross (pinned), Ratcliffe, Didcot. © image — replace before publishing.

name: Baldwinstown Castle
blurb: Your ancestors built it in 1195; burned after the Cromwellian war in 1649 and the family dispossessed, but it still stands.

name: Deserted castle overlooking Wexford town
blurb: Unnamed in the notes; deserted but beautiful, above the town.

name: Birthplace of John Barry
blurb: "Father of the US Navy," born about three miles from Baldwinstown.

name: Donadea Castle, Donadea Forest Park
blurb: Ruined castle in a forest park; good any time of year.

name: Moore Hall
blurb: Built 1792–95, burned by the IRA in 1923 during the Civil War; it and its surrounding 80 acres are now a national park.

name: Kylemore Abbey
blurb: The lakeside Gothic abbey.

name: Rock of Cashel
blurb: The great ecclesiastical fortress on the rock.

name: Cahir Castle
blurb: One of Ireland's largest, best-preserved castles.

name: Dublin
blurb: Whiskey, castles and gardens (the Dublin → Cashel → Cahir run).

name: The Famine Memorial, Dublin
blurb: Bronze figures on Custom House Quay remembering the Great Famine (1845–49), which halved the population through death and emigration.

name: The Dark Hedges
blurb: The tunnel of interlaced beeches.

name: Cliffs of Moher
blurb: UNESCO sea cliffs on the edge of the Burren; ~9 miles long, rising to 702 ft, with O'Brien's Tower; ~1.6 million visitors a year.

name: Inis Meáin, Aran Islands
blurb: Atlantic waves breaking against the cliffs.

name: Burg Eltz
blurb: 12th c.; the storybook valley castle.

name: Marksburg
blurb: 12th–15th c.; the only Rhine hill-castle never destroyed.

name: Reichsburg Cochem
blurb: 19th-c. rebuild on a Moselle hilltop.

name: Stahleck Castle
blurb: 12th c., above Bacharach on the Rhine.

name: Lichtenstein Castle
blurb: 19th-c. cliff-edge "fairy-tale castle of Württemberg."

name: Heidelberg Castle
blurb: 13th–17th c.; the great red-sandstone ruin above the Neckar.

name: Hohenzollern Castle
blurb: 19th-c.; ancestral seat of the Prussian kings, on a lone peak.

name: Sigmaringen Castle
blurb: Dominates the town skyline; rebuilt after an 1893 fire, only the medieval towers remain.

name: Hohenschwangau
blurb: 19th c.; the yellow castle where Ludwig II grew up.

name: Neuschwanstein
blurb: Ludwig II's fantasy castle; the icon.

name: Mespelbrunn Castle
blurb: Moated Renaissance water-castle in the Spessart.

name: Herrenchiemsee
blurb: Ludwig II's Versailles on an island; the Hall of Mirrors (1879–81) is longer than the original.

name: Schloss Drachenburg
blurb: 19th-c. neo-Gothic palace above the Rhine.

name: Vischering Castle
blurb: 16th-c. moated castle.

name: Wartburg
blurb: 12th–15th c.; where Luther translated the New Testament.

name: Marienburg Castle
blurb: 19th-c. neo-Gothic, brooding under storm skies.

name: Schloss Moritzburg
blurb: Baroque moated hunting palace.

name: Schwerin Castle
blurb: Romantic island palace reflected in the Schweriner See.

name: Burg Frankenstein
blurb: The "spooky" hill castle of legend.

name: Cologne Cathedral
blurb: 632 years to complete; "it needs more than a mere opinion to erect a Gothic cathedral."

name: Dresden
blurb: The Zwinger, Frauenkirche and old town rebuilt from WWII ruins, much from scratch in the last 20 years.

name: Rothenburg ob der Tauber
blurb: Fairy-tale walled town; one of only four in Germany with fully intact city walls.

name: Boltanski, The Missing House
blurb: 24 nameplates on two firewalls at Große Hamburger Straße, naming residents of a building destroyed in WWII.

name: Berlin Street Chess
blurb: The open-air chess scene.

name: Museum Sinsheim
blurb: Vast aviation/tech museum (planes, tanks, cars); sister site at Speyer.

name: Computer Museum (Heinz Nixdorf MuseumsForum, Paderborn)
blurb: The world's largest computer museum.

name: Prague Astronomical Clock (1410)
blurb: The world's oldest operating astronomical clock; was misfiled in the Germany screenshots.

name: German Observation Tower, Guernsey
blurb: A WWII German fortification, so it belongs with the UK/British Isles set, not Germany.

name: Guggenheim Bilbao
blurb: Frank Gehry's titanium-clad museum on the Nervión; Louise Bourgeois's Maman spider out front. Basque Country.

name: Alhambra, Granada
blurb: Quranic texts, Arabic poetry and calligraphy carved into the pillars and arches ~800 years ago.

name: Temple Expiatori del Sagrat Cor, Tibidabo (Barcelona)
blurb: Crypt 1903–11, main church 1915–51; by Catalan architect Enric Sagnier. Crowns the Tibidabo hill over the city.

name: Sevilla — Plaza de España
blurb: Stood in for Naboo in Star Wars: Attack of the Clones.

name: Medina of Essaouira (formerly Mogador)
blurb: A rare late-18th-century fortified town built on European military-architecture principles in a North African setting; UNESCO-listed.

name: The Louvre
blurb: Aerial with I. M. Pei's glass pyramid, Arc de Triomphe beyond.

name: Musée d'Orsay
blurb: Impressionist collection in the former Gare d'Orsay (Beaux-Arts station, 1898–1900), French art 1848–1914.

name: Lionel Walden, The Docks of Cardiff (1894)
blurb: A painting you flagged at the Musée d'Orsay.

name: David, The Coronation of Napoleon
blurb: The enormous canvas behind the Mona Lisa at the Louvre ("infinitely cooler," per the post).

name: Monet's Water Lilies, Musée de l'Orangerie
blurb: Monet's Water Lilies at the Musée de l'Orangerie.

name: Monet's house & gardens, Giverny
blurb: The interior of Claude Monet's home (Normandy, ~75 km NW of Paris).

name: Père Lachaise Cemetery
blurb: "The greatest cemetery on Earth"; resting place of Morrison, Chopin, Piaf, Wilde.

name: La Samaritaine
blurb: Art Nouveau department store, opened 1869, closed 2005 for safety, reopened beautifully restored in 2021.

name: Galeries Lafayette Haussmann
blurb: The Belle Époque glass-and-iron dome (Georges Chedanne, 1912).

name: Moulin Rouge
blurb: Colorized from a 1914 original.

name: Le Passe-Muraille (Walker-Through-Walls)
blurb: Jean Marais sculpture, 1989. (Source said catacombs; it's actually in Montmartre, Place Marcel-Aymé.)

name: Jardin de la Roseraie (Roseraie du Val-de-Marne, L'Haÿ-les-Roses)
Les Espaces d'Abraxas, Noisy-le-Grand
blurb: Ricardo Bofill brutalist housing estate, 1982.

name: Les Arènes de Picasso, Noisy-le-Grand
blurb: Manuel Núñez Yanowsky's disc-shaped social-housing complex, 1984; central sculpture by Miguel Berrocal.

name: Tour Saint-Gobain, La Défense
blurb: 165 m, 44-floor sustainable tower by Valode & Pistre.

name: La Cité du Cinéma, Saint-Denis
blurb: Film studios; the flying cab from The Fifth Element (image is from a making-of). Gaumont, the world's oldest film company (1895), connects here.

name: Puddles in Paris
blurb: Your reflection-photography series; Haussmann façades and figures mirrored in rain.

name: Loire Valley (Amboise / Touraine)
blurb: Château country.

name: Château d'Amboise — tomb of Leonardo da Vinci
blurb: Château d'Amboise — tomb of Leonardo da Vinci.

name: Château de Brissac
blurb: Tallest château in France; legend of the "Dame Verte," Charlotte de Valois, said to haunt the halls seeking her killer.

name: Château de la Mothe-Chandeniers
blurb: The romantic moated ruin in the west (Vienne); a crowd-funded restoration.

name: Brittany (region)
blurb: Northwesternmost France; rugged coast, the blush-toned Pink Granite Coast, walled Saint-Malo, chic Dinard, abundant prehistoric menhirs. Capital Rennes.

name: The Trembling Rock (Roche Tremblante), Huelgoat
blurb: A 132-ton granite block balanced so anyone can rock it slightly.

name: Bordeaux
blurb: The city; cathedral and old streets.

name: Librairie Mollat, Bordeaux
blurb: France's oldest independent bookshop.

name: Lascaux Cave, Dordogne
blurb: Upper Paleolithic cave art (c. 17,000–15,000 BC); ~600 paintings, mostly horses, plus ~1,400 engravings.

name: Argenton-sur-Creuse
blurb: Riverside village in the Indre, "where history meets the good life."

name: Bourges Cathedral
blurb: Gardens and medieval streets (the Bette Davis / Petrified Forest reference).

name: Carcassonne
blurb: Fairy-tale fortified city, one of Europe's best-preserved medieval defensive sites.

name: Grand Marnier — Château de Bourg-Charente, Cognac
blurb: The distillery on the Charente; occasional guided visits and tastings by appointment.

name: Saint-Paul-de-Vence
blurb: One of the oldest medieval towns on the Riviera; home of poet Jacques Prévert.

name: Relics of Mary Magdalene, Basilique Sainte-Marie-Madeleine, Saint-Maximin-la-Sainte-Baume
blurb: The gold-ensconced skull, "one of the most precious relics in all Christendom."

name: Gaumont / Louis de Funès Museum, Saint-Raphaël
blurb: Museum to France's favourite actor; tie-in with Gaumont, the world's oldest film studio.

name: Unité d'Habitation, Marseille
blurb: Le Corbusier's 1940s "vertical city," UNESCO-listed 2016.

name: Bibliothèque Méjanes, Aix-en-Provence
blurb: Façade shaped like three giant books: Camus's The Stranger, Saint-Exupéry's The Little Prince, Molière's The Imaginary Invalid.

name: Colmar (Alsace)
blurb: Storybook half-timbered town.

name: Art Nouveau storefront, Douai
blurb: Ornate glass shopfront added 1904, by Albert Pépe.

name: Château de Pierrefonds (Oise)
blurb: Viollet-le-Duc's romantic reconstruction.

name: Cadaver Tomb of René of Chalon, Bar-le-Duc
blurb: Ligier Richier's transi (c. 1544–57): a standing, skinless "living corpse" raising its heart toward heaven.

name: Notre-Dame-du-Haut, Ronchamp
blurb: Le Corbusier's pilgrimage chapel.

name: Auditorium Maurice-Ravel, Lyon
blurb: Concert hall dedicated to Ravel's legacy.

name: St Peter's Basilica & Square
blurb: Shot at sunset; the basilica faces west, unusual among churches.

name: St Peter's Square obelisk
blurb: A 4,000-year-old Egyptian obelisk brought from Alexandria by Caligula in 37 AD.

name: Vatican — Gallery of Maps
blurb: The gilded map corridor; "I must be dreaming."

name: Sistine Chapel ceiling
blurb: Michelangelo, 1508–.

name: Pericle Fazzini, The Resurrection (1977)
blurb: Colossal bronze in the Paul VI Audience Hall, Vatican (20 m wide).

name: The Pantheon door
blurb: The bronze portal, c. 115 AD, still turning on its hinges after ~2,000 years.

name: Sant'Ignazio di Loyola — the false dome
blurb: Andrea Pozzo's trompe-l'oeil "fake arched ceiling," 1685.

name: Bernini, David, Galleria Borghese
blurb: Bernini's David in the Galleria Borghese.

name: Salvador Dalí at San Marcello al Corso
blurb: Salvador Dalí work at the church of San Marcello al Corso.

name: Mausoleum of Hadrian (Castel Sant'Angelo)
blurb: Towering cylindrical fortress in Parco Adriano.

name: La Fenice opera house
blurb: "The Phoenix," rebuilt from fire more than once; a Baroque jewel.

name: Venice (the city)
blurb: Virtually unchanged since the Middle Ages; gondolas licensed since 1094.

name: Venice Carnival
blurb: Carnevale costumes.

name: Lorenzo Quinn, Support (2017)
blurb: Giant hands rising from the Grand Canal.

name: August Lovati, San Marco
blurb: Painting (1852–1921).

name: Scaligero Castle, Sirmione
blurb: 14th-c. lakeside fortress on Lake Garda.

name: Siena
blurb: "One of the most beautiful medieval cities on Earth."

name: San Gimignano
blurb: The "Town of Fine Towers."

name: Monteriggioni
blurb: Italy's most intact walled medieval town; Dante used its turrets to picture the giants ringing the Inferno.

name: Volterra
blurb: Tuscan hill town of medieval alleys.

name: Florence
blurb: Birthplace of the Renaissance.

name: Santa Maria del Fiore — floor mosaic
blurb: The intricate marble inlay of Florence's cathedral.

name: Giambologna's Appennine Colossus
blurb: The giant stone mountain-god (late 1500s), Villa Demidoff, Pratolino.

name: Palaia
blurb: Small Tuscan hill town.

name: Amalfi Coast
blurb: Positano, Amalfi, Ravello, Sorrento, plus quieter towns (Cetara, Vietri sul Mare).

name: Positano
blurb: The cliffside icon of the coast.

name: Teatro di San Carlo, Naples
blurb: The opera house.

name: Valle dei Mulini (Abandoned Mill), Sorrento
blurb: Ruined mill swallowed by green in a ravine.

name: Valley of the Temples, Agrigento
blurb: The great Doric temples; most important vestige of classical culture in Sicily.

name: Milan — the Duomo
blurb: Caught right before a thunderstorm.

name: Piazzale Loreto, Milan
blurb: Where Mussolini's body was displayed in 1945; the spot is now a McDonald's.

name: Bologna — the towers
blurb: "Manhattan of the Middle Ages"; ~180 towers in the 12th c., the tallest 97 m still standing (30 of 100 remain).

name: Castello di Vezio, Lake Como
blurb: The white "ghost" sculptures in the grounds.

name: Torno, Lake Como
blurb: Lakeside village.

name: Staglieno / Monumental Cemetery, Genoa
blurb: Monumental funerary sculpture; one grave was used on the cover of Joy Division's "Love Will Tear Us Apart."

name: Castello di Torrechiara, Parma
blurb: Built 1448–60 for Bianca Pellegrini; grotesque frescoes by Cesare Baglione, full of fantastical beasts and warriors.

name: Castel del Monte, Apulia
blurb: Frederick II's perfectly symmetrical octagonal castle, 1240s.

name: Rocca Calascio, Abruzzo
blurb: One of the highest fortresses in the Apennines, stunning in winter.

name: Crypt of San Magno, Anagni
blurb: Fully frescoed medieval crypt in the "City of Popes," SE of Rome.

name: "Celebrating Joy Division," Liguria (Sestri Levante area)
blurb: Flagged: exact spot uncertain from the copy; a Joy Division tribute.

name: Pompeii / Mount Vesuvius
blurb: The Roman city frozen in time by the AD 79 eruption; well-preserved streets beneath the volcano.

name: Villa del Balbianello, Lenno (Lake Como)
blurb: Terraced villa and gardens; a Star Wars Naboo location (also Casino Royale). Its garden holds a ~140-year-old Holm Oak.

name: Cinque Terre
blurb: The five coastal villages; the Monterosso al Mare → Vernazza walk is rewarding, if tiring.

name: Pavillon Le Corbusier
blurb: 1967 steel-and-enamel art museum, Le Corbusier's last building, dedicated to his work in Zürich-Seefeld.

name: Sagrada Família
blurb: Gaudí's unfinished basilica, under construction since 1882 and a UNESCO World Heritage masterpiece of Catalan modernism.

name: Jim Morrison's Grave
blurb: The Doors frontman Jim Morrison's grave in Père Lachaise Cemetery, a pilgrimage site for rock fans worldwide.
